coredata.dc:title: High-performance pseudo-capacitor energy storage device based on a hollow-structured copper sulfide nanoflower and carbon quantum dot nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Quan, Yiling
Wang, Guoxiang
Lu, Lu
Wang, Zhixin
Xu, Hongfeng
Liu, Sa
Wang, Dazhi
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal sulfides are widely used in high-performance energy storage equipment due to its excellent electrochemical activity and electrical conductivity. In this study, we introduce a carbon quantum dot (CQD)-doped hollow CuS composite (CuS@CQDs) as a novel electrode material for advanced asymmetric supercapacitors through one-step solvothermal synthesis and an impregnation bonding method. The CuS@CQDs composites with uniform and hierarchical porous architecture possess high specific area and low specific resistance, which can facilitate the transport of electrolytes and electrons. The as-prepared CuS@CQDs-ICM electrode exhibits a maximum specific capacitance of 920.5 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 and an energy density of 44.19 W h kg−1 at a power density of 397.75 W kg−1 with an excellent cycling stability of 92.8% after 10,000 cycles. The satisfactory electrochemical performance can be attributed to the unique nanoflower-like hollow structure of the CuS@CQDs composites and the synergy between CuS and CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitors
Carbon quantum dot
Nanoflower-like
Hollow structure
One-step solvothermal synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure of CQDs and CuS@CQDs complexes.
XRD patterns of CQDs, CuS, and CuS@CQDs-SSM and CuS@CQDs-ICM composites.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs; (b)–(d) TEM images of the CQDs at different magnifications.
FESEM images of (a)–(b) CuS, (c)–(d) CuS@CQDs-ICM, and (e)–(f) CuS@CQDs-SSM at different magnifications.
(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distribution of the CQDs, CuS,CuS@CQDs-ICM, and CuS@CQDs-SSM composites.

(a) CV curves at different scan rates from 5 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1; (b) GCD curves of the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites under various current densities; (c) CV curves at 10 mV s−1; (d) GCD curves at 0.5 A g−1; (e)–(f) Specific capacitance values and EIS data of the CuS, CuS@CQDs-SSM, and CuS@CQDs-ICM composites.

(a) Schematic of the assembled CuS@CQDs-ICM//ACasymmetric device; (b) CV curves of the AC and CuS@CQDs-ICM electrodes at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1; (c) CV curves at different scan rates; (d) GCD curves at different current densities; (e) Ragone plot of the CuS@CQDs-ICM//AC ASC compared with other reported data; (f) Cycling performance of the CuS@CQDs-ICM//AC ASC evaluated at 5 A g−1 for 10,000 cycles.
Structural parameter of CQDs, CuS, CuS@CQDs-ICM and CuS@CQDs-SSM composites.

head.ce:title: High-performance pseudo-capacitor energy storage device based on a hollow-structured copper sulfide nanoflower and carbon quantum dot nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Light Industry & Chemical Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, 116034, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Light Industry & Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Light Industry & Chemical Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory of New Energy Battery, Dalian Jiaotong University, Dalian, 116028, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory of New Energy Battery

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory of New Energy Battery, Dalian Jiaotong University, Dalian 116028, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou, 221116, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou 221116, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal sulfides are widely used in high-performance energy storage equipment due to its excellent electrochemical activity and electrical conductivity. In this study, we introduce a carbon quantum dot (CQD)-doped hollow CuS composite (CuS@CQDs) as a novel electrode material for advanced asymmetric supercapacitors through one-step solvothermal synthesis and an impregnation bonding method. The CuS@CQDs composites with uniform and hierarchical porous architecture possess high specific area and low specific resistance, which can facilitate the transport of electrolytes and electrons. The as-prepared CuS@CQDs-ICM electrode exhibits a maximum specific capacitance of 920.5 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 and an energy density of 44.19 W h kg−1 at a power density of 397.75 W kg−1 with an excellent cycling stability of 92.8% after 10,000 cycles. The satisfactory electrochemical performance can be attributed to the unique nanoflower-like hollow structure of the CuS@CQDs composites and the synergy between CuS and CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors (SCs) are indispensable components of energy storage equipment; these components are widely used in modern electronic devices, such as transportation, military and aerospace, portable electronics, and memory devices [1–3]. As a new type of power supply, SCs differ from batteries in terms of charge storage mechanism. The surface charge storage mechanisms of SCs contribute to the higher power density, faster charge and discharge rates, pollution-free operation, and longer cycle life in comparison with batteries [4–6]. However, low energy density hinders the practical application of SCs.

SCs are divided into symmetrical capacitor and an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC). The latter possesses high specific capacitance because of the reversible redox reactions [7–10]. Recently, researchers have developed advanced electrode materials to obtain an excellent electrochemical performance, [11] which depends on physical properties, such as the structure, type, and morphology of the obtained electrode material. Substantial research on the design and synthesis of effective electrode materials with unique structures has been conducted [12]. In recent years, metal oxides (e.g., Co3O4 and NiO) and hydroxides (e.g., Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2) have been widely used as electrode materials for ASC due to their high theoretical specific capacitance and low cost. H. Hwang et al. synthesized core–shell Ni–MnO2 hybrid foams through the structure-guided combustion wave method for supercapacitor electrodes and achieveda satisfactory specific capacitance of 660 F g−1 [13]. Li et al. fabricated amorphous Ni(OH)2–MnO2 array through a template route; the array exhibited a high specific capacitance of 14.7 F cm−2 at 2 mA cm−2 [14]. However, the low electrical conductivity of transition metal oxide is detrimental to fast electronic transport and cycling ability, thus limiting the further application of these materials in the SC electrode [15,16]. Therefore, the development of an electrode material with improved conductivity and high stability during redox reactions is necessary to address the above mentioned issues.

In the past decade, transition metal sulfides have received extensive attention due to their excellent electrical conductivity and electrochemical activity [17,18]. Moreover, various transition metal sulfides have diverse structures, abundant valence states, and large lattice distances compared with related oxides. These characteristic increase the performance of rapid and reversible redox reactions [19]. In previous works, CoS, NiS, and CuS have been employed as electrode materials, which exhibited excellent electrochemical performance [20–22]. Among these materials, CuS is one of the most promising because of its large theoretical capacity, low cost, high production efficiency, and environment friendliness. However, pure CuS results in low electrical conductivity and short cycle life [23]. Therefore, CuS should be modified using a material with good conductivity to improve its electrochemical performance. Recently, carbon-based materials, such as activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, and graphene, have been widely used as carriers for the manufacture of metal S–C hybrid materials due to their excellent electrical conductivity, high specific surface area, and porous properties. Studies have reported that the introduction of conductive carbon-based composites improves the conductivity of S2−-based electrode materials, such as PIM/CuS@CNT, CuS-AC, and CoS-AC. For example, Tang et al. obtained monodispersed hollow spindle-like nanosphere and tetrapod nanorod MnS nanocrystals via a facile template-free hydrothermal process, which further demonstrated excellent electrochemical performance (704.5 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1) [24]. Wang et al. obtained strawberry-like Ni3S2@Co9S8 hetero-nanoparticle composites by a cross-linking and in-situ sulfuration strategy, which also exhibited excellent electrochemical performance (1970.5 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1) [25]. However, the abovementioned carbon-based materials are expensive, hence, low-cost ones have been developed. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of zero-dimensional nanocarbon material, are spherical nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size, with surfaces that are rich in functional groups (e.g., carboxylic acids) that provide ample opportunity for surface functionalization. The versatility of CQDs is a great advantage for electrochemical applications. When combined with metal sulfide, CQDs act as an electron donor and acceptor so that the final composite exhibits good electrical conductivity. To the best of our knowledge, a few studies on asymmetric supercapacitors with hollow CuS composites (CuS@CQDs) have been conducted [26,27]. For example, Zhao et al. fabricated RGO/CuS composite by solvothermal reaction methods, showing a high specific capacitance of 906 F g−1 [28]. Li et al. synthesized Cu7S4/C using a two-step route sulfidation from a MOF template, presenting superior 321.9 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 [29].

In this work, we developed a novel CQD-doped CuS hollow nanoflower-like composite using a simple one-step solvothermal synthesis method (SSM) and impregnation combined method (ICM). The specific capacitance of the synthesized CuS@CQDs-ICM composite (920.5 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1) is higher than that of pure CuS (369.9 F g−1). The AC and CuS@CQDs-ICM electrodes are respectively used as the negative and positive electrodes to assemble an asymmetric supercapacitor. The assembled device exhibited a maximum energy density of 44.19 W h kg−1 at a power density of 397.75 W kg−1. Moreover, the specific capacitance reached 92.8% after 10,000 charge/discharge cycles. The satisfactory electrochemical performance of the ASC device could be ascribed to the synergistic effect between CQDs and CuS, which significantly improved the electron transport properties of the material.

Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO)3·3H2O, ≥99.0%), sulfur sublimed (≥99.5%), ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2, ≥99.0%), potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥99.0%), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, ≥99.0%), actived carbon (AC) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, ≥99.0%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Anhydrous citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥99.5%) was obtained from Aladdin. All the reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout this work.

Synthesis of CQDs: CQDs were prepared in accordance with a method from a previous work [30]. C6H8O7 (4.204 g, 0.02 mol) and C2H8N2 (1.34 mL) were mixed into 40 mL of deionized water and stirred for 10 min. Then, the mixed solution was uniformly dispersed through ultrasonic treatment for 5 min. Afterward, the mixture was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. When the hydrothermal reaction was completed, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature to obtain a brown–yellow CQD solution.

Synthesis of CuS@CQDs-ICM and CuS@CQDs-SSM composites: The CuS@CQDs-SSM composite was prepared using single-step solvothermal synthesis, where 0.24 g of Cu(NO)3 and 0.36 g of CTAB in ethylene glycol (60 mL) were stirred for 1 h before adding 3 mL of CQDs and mixing continuously for 2 h. After mixed, 0.05 g of S was added into the above mixture, and the final mixture was poured into the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 15 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the samples were filtered and successively washed several times with distilled water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C for 12h in a vacuum oven. Then, the pure CuS was synthesized using the abovementioned procedure but without CQDs. The CuS@CQDs-ICM composite was obtained by immersing the synthesized CuS in the CQD solution for 12h hand freeze drying at −50 °C for 12 h.

The microstructure and morphology information were characterized using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) (Hitachi S4300) with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (OxfoView Article Onlinerd, UK) and transmission electron microscope (Hitachi H9000NAR). The detailed structure information of the obtained electrode materials was further investigated through X-ray diffraction (XRD), Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) theory, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and a fluorescence spectrometer (LS-55). XRD was used to identify the crystal structure of the samples at a Cu Kα radiation of λ = 1.5406 Å at 4 kV. The chemical composition was analyzed using a ThermoEscalab 250 XPS using Al Kα X-ray as the excitation source. The specific surface area was determined using the BET equation, and the pore size distribution was studied through the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) analysis. Moreover, the steady state photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) with Xe lamp as the excitation source.

The electrochemical performances of the samples were inspected through cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance (EIS) at an electrochemical workstation (PARSTAT 4000, Germany). The electrochemical measurements of the samples were gathered using the typical three-electrode system that utilizes the obtained material, Pt, and calomel electrodes as the working, counter, and reference electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was prepared by mixing the as-prepared active materials, acetylene black, and polytetrafluoroethylene at a weight ratio of 8:1:1, fabricating the mixture into a thin film, and then pressing between two Ni foams. All electrochemical measurements were performed in a 6 M-KOH aqueous electrolyte solution at room temperature.

The electrochemical property of the ASC was investigated using the CuS and CuS@CQDs composites as the positive electrode and the AC as the negative electrode. The specific capacitance C (F g−1), energy density E (W h kg−1) and power density P (W kg−1) of the ASC were calculated from the discharge curves using the following equations:
(1)
C =
I × Δ t
m × Δ V
(2)
E =
0.5 ×
C m
×
(
Δ V
)
2
3.6
(3)
P =
E × 3600
Δ t
Where I, Δt and ΔV are defined as the current density (A g−1), discharge time (s) and effective voltage (V) excluding IR drop, respectively. m is the mass of the active electrode material (g).

Fig. 1 shows the fabrication procedure of the CQDs and CuS@CQDs complexes. The CQDs are prepared through the hydrothermal method (Fig. 1a). A reaction is first formed by the condensation of C6H8O7 and C2H8N2 according to the formation of polymer-like CQDs, which is then carbonized to form the CQDs. The CuS@CQDs-SSM composite is synthesized through simple solvent thermal synthesis. Cu(NO3)2 and sublimed S are used as the precursors for Cu and S, and the CQDs are used as the carbon source before synthesizing using a one-step process. The CuS@CQDs-ICM composite is prepared through electrostatic adsorption in which numerous CQDs are adsorbed on the surface of pure CuS.

The crystalline-phase characteristics of the as-synthesized CQDs, CuS, CuS@CQDs-SSM, and CuS@CQDs-ICM are analyzed using XRD patterns (Fig. 2 ). The XRD diffraction peak of CQDs is consistent with the findings of previous works in which a broad peak centered at 24.08° is observed due to highly disordered carbon atoms [31]. The major diffraction peaks of the CuS are observed at 2θ = 27.28°, 27.62°, 29.27°, 31.91°, 32.90°, 38.96°, 43.36°, 44.57°, 47.88°, 52.83°, 57.24°, 59.44°,and 67.49°, which correspond to (100), (101), (102), (103), (006), (105), (106), (008), (110), (108), (202), (116), and (118), respectively. The peak of the CuS@CQDs-ICM and CuS@CQDs-SSM composites at 24.08° can be ascribed to the CQDs’ graphitic plane, which is strongly attached to the CuS nanoparticles in the hybrid.

The fluorescence spectra and the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CQDs’ aqueous solution are displayed in Fig. 3 . Excitation-dependent emission can be clearly observed, and all emission maxima are centered in the visible region of the spectrum (Fig. 3a).The emission peaks shift to the high wavelengths as the excitation wavelengths increase from 330 nm to 420 nm, thereby indicating the effective preparation of the CQDs. This excitation-dependent PL behavior is common in fluorescent carbon materials [32]. Fig. 3b–d shows the TEM images of the three different resolutions of CQDs. The CQDs are almost uniform in size, monodispersed, and possess spherical morphologies. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows that the average diameter of the CQDs is approximately 4 nm. The spacing of the adjacent lattice planes for CQDs nanoparticles are 0.33 and 0.21 nm, which are very close to the (002) and (100) diffraction planes of graphite, respectively. This finding indicates that the prepared CQDs have a high degree of graphitization [33].

The morphology of the materials is obtained using FESEM. Fig. 4 a and b illustrate the CuS samples at different magnifications. CuS shows a flower-like hollow structure with uniform distribution and size (diameter is approximately 1.3 μm) (Fig. 4a). Fig. 4b is the enlarged view of Fig. 4a; it displays the supported hollow structure of the obtained pure CuS. The spherical shell of the CuS hollow nanoflowers is approximately 230 nm, which is formed by stacking many nanosheets. This stacked structure allows the development of pores on the surface of the CuS flower-like nanospheres and in turn facilitates the transport of electrolyte ions during electrochemical testing; consequently, electrochemical performance is improved [34]. Fig. 4c and d presents the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the CuS@CQDs-ICM composite. The introduction of CQDs does not change the uniform distribution and uniformity of the obtained materials (Fig. 4c). Moreover, the SEM images in Fig. 4d confirm that the surface of the CuS nanoflower sphere is covered with a layer of material, indicating that the CQDs have been doped into the composite. The SEM images of theCuS@CQDs-SSM composites are presented in Fig. 4e and f. Compared with the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites, the nanosheets on the surface of the CuS@CQDs-SSM flower-shaped hollow nanospheres are more compact. The reduction in the surface porosity of the CuS@CQDs-SSM composites might affect the transport of electrolyte ions. The elemental distribution and composition of the CuS@CQDs-ICM composite are further investigated using the EDS mapping spectrum (Fig. S1a). The synthesized CuS@CQDs-ICM holds the corresponding elements of Cu, S, N, O, and C (Fig. S1b). The inset table lists the weight and atomic percentage of the elements. Fig. S2 depicts typical TEM image of the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites and shows that the diameter of the CuS@CQDs-ICM nanoflower is approximately 1.3 μm. The SEM images reveal numerous pores on the surface of the composite material. Bright spots are evident in the typical selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset of Fig. S2), indicating the single crystalline properties of the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites.

The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the samples are analyzed using N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distribution analysis. The specific surface area, pore volume, and average pore size of the samples obtained through BET analysis are summarized in Table 1 . The pure CuS maintains a specific surface area of 22.8 m2 g−1 and a concentrated pore size distribution (0–10 nm). Compared with pure CuS composites, the CQDs and CuS@CQDs-ICM composites exhibit higher specific surface area and pore size distribution (0–30 nm). The CQD, CuS@CQDs-ICM, and CuS@CQDs-SSM composites demonstrate a typical type-IV isotherm and a H3-type hysteresis loop at a relative pressure (P/P
0 ) between 0.4 and 0.9, indicating an abundance of mesopores (Fig. 5 a). This hierarchical porous architecture can provide abundant electroactive sites and allow full contact with the electrolyte to facilitate electron/ion embedding, resulting in excellent electrochemical energy storage properties [35].

The elemental composition and electronic structure of the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites are further evaluated using the obtained XPS spectra. The peaks centered at 929.0, 159.5, 282.4, 529.9 and 398.1 eV in the wide-scan XPS image (Fig. S3a) correspond to Cu 2p, S 2p, C 1s, O 2p, and N 1s, respectively. This correspondence confirms the presence of Cu, S, C, O, and N in the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites. In the high-resolution spectrum of Cu 2p (Fig. S3b), the peaks at 929.1 and 948.9 eV are assigned to the binding energies of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively, and the two small satellite peaks observed at 930.8 and 950.9 eV correspond to the Cu2+ state. In the S 2p spectrum (Fig. S3c), the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 peaks appear at binding energies of 159.3 and 160.7 eV, respectively, whereas the peak positioned at 162.1 eV is associated with the Cu–S bonding. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. S3d) exhibits two main peaks at 282.3, and 286.5 eV, corresponding to the C–C and C–OH groups, respectively. Based on the O1s spectrum (Fig. S3e), several C–O, O–H, and CO groups are present on the surface of the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites, which can supply the scores of active sites. Detailed information on CuS@CQDs-ICM composites about the N species can be deduced from the N1s spectrum (Fig. S3f); the peak is resolved into three different regions centered at 398.1, 399.5, and 405.4 eV, which correspond to the pyridinic-N, quaternary-N, and oxides-N in the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites, respectively. The XPS data reveal the existence and uniform distribution of the CQDs on the surface of the CuS nanoflower.

As previously stated, the electrochemical performance of the proposed material is investigated through CV, GCD, and EIS in a 6M-KOH electrolyte using a three-electrode system. Fig. 6 a shows the CV curves of the CuS@CQDs-ICM electrode at scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mV s−1 in a potential window from −0.1 V to 0.5 V. The CV curves exhibit an excellent central symmetry, indicating that the electrode possesses outstanding reversibility. The redox peak of CV shifts by a certain amount as the scanning rate increases, which can be ascribed to the occurrence of minute polarization during the charging and discharging processes. The GCD measurements are conducted at different current densities ranging from 0.5 A g−1 to 10 A g−1 at a potential range of 0–0.4 V. Such measurements are conducted to estimate the pseudocapacitive behavior of the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites (Fig. 6b). The symmetrical charge and discharge curves indicate the excellent reversibility of the CuS@CQDs-ICM electrode. In addition, the existence of plateaus at approximately 0.2 V in the charging curve suggests the typical pseudocapacitive behavior of the Faraday redox reaction. For comparison, the CV curves of the CuS, CuS@CQDs-SSM, and CuS@CQDs-ICM electrodes at a scan rate of 10 mVs−1 are obtained. The enclosed area of the CuS@CQDs-ICM and CuS@CQDs-SSM composites is larger than that of the bare CuS nanoflower, suggesting that the coating of the CQDs on the surface of the latter promotes the redox reaction of CuS and contributes additional pseudocapacitance (Fig. 6c).The capacity storage performance is then evaluated via GCD measurement, which is conducted at a potential range of 0–0.4 V and current density of 0.5 A g−1 (Fig. 6d). The GCD curves of the CuS, CuS@CQDs-SSM, and CuS@CQDs-ICM electrodes demonstrate a symmetric curve with a discharge plateau located at 0.20–0.25 V, signifying reversible faradaic reactions. The CV and GCD curves of the CuS@CQDs-SSM and pure CuS composites are provided in Fig. S4 and S5. The specific capacitance of the samples is calculated from the GCD curves in accordance with Eq. (1). The CuS@CQDs-ICM composites demonstrate a specific capacitance of 920.5 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, which is higher than that of CuS@CQDs-SSM (434.5 F g−1) and CuS (369.9 F g−1) (Fig. 6e). The electrochemical performance of the three electrodes is further investigated through EIS analysis. Fig. 6f shows the resultant Nyquist plots, which comprise a partial semicircle in the high-frequency region and a straight line in the low-frequencyone. The semicircle loop in the former reflects the charge transfer resistance (R ct) [36]. The Rct values of CuS@CQDs-ICM, CuS@CQDs-SSM, and CuS are 0.15, 0.13, and 0.18 Ω, respectively. The CuS@CQDs-ICM and CuS@CQDs-SSM electrodes obtain a lower R ct value compared with pure CuS, implying that the fast charge transfer on the electrode surface is caused by the synergistic effect between CuS and CQDs. In the latter, the larger the slope of the straight line, the greater the electrolyte diffusion rate of the electrolyte [37].

The basic electrochemical properties of the CuS-CQDs-ICM electrode in aqueous electrolytes must be understood; thus, the cathodic and anodic current densities, as functions of scan rate, are evaluated to analyze the origin of the enhanced capacitance performance. A linear relationship between the peak current and the scan rate is observed (Fig. S6a). The slight change in peak separation and high current response indicate the existence of a capacitive charge storage process. The kinetic behavior of the electrodes is also investigated to analyze the capacity contribution (Figs.S6a–S6d). The area under the CV curve represents the total capacitance contribution of the surface and the diffusion control process. The dependence of the current response (i) on the scan rate (v) can be qualitatively expressed as equation:
(4)
i = a
v b

The value of b is close to 0.5 during the process of diffusion control and approaches 1.0 during the process dominated by the surface capacitance [38]. Therefore, the electrochemical process of diffusion or surface control can be distinguished in accordance with the value of b. As shown in Fig. S6b, the b values of the cathode and anode peaks are 0.826 and 0.615, respectively, indicating that the charge storage undergoes a surface and diffusion control process. The diffusion and surface control charge storage processes are quantified to investigate the storage mechanism of the charges in the CuS@CQDs-ICM composites according to the following equation:
(5)
i
( V )
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
Where i is the current at a given potential, v is the scan rate, and k1 and k2 are suitable values. Herein, k1 v and k2 v 1/2 define the fractions of current contributed by surface and diffusion control processes, respectively. The shaded area in the CV curve in Fig. S6c represents the capacitance contribution of the surface control process. At a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, the surface capacitance contribution accounts for approximately 74.87% of the total capacitance, whereas that of the diffusion capacitance is approximately 25.13%, accounting for the overall storage of charge. Fig. S6d illustrates the charge storage contribution rates at various scan rates. The contribution of surface capacitance charge increases with the increase scan rate, indicating that the capacitive charge storage process dominates a wide range of scan rates.

Fig. 7 a shows a schematic of the assembled CuS@CQDs-ICM//AC asymmetric device. The CV curves of the AC and CuS@CQDs-ICM electrodes at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 7b. The voltage window of AC is from −1 V to 0 V, which shows a typical feature of electrical double-layer capacitance. The CuS@CQDs-ICM electrode exhibits an excellent pseudocapacitive performance in the positive potential window of −0.1–0.5 V. The CV and GCD information are provided in the supporting information (Fig. S7). The shape of the CV curves at various scanning rate indicate that the capacitance is the result of the combined contribution of the pseudo- and double-layer capacitances (Fig. 7c). To evaluate the charge–discharge capacity, the CuS@CQDs-ICM//AC ASC device is characterized using a GCD technology. The GCD curves indicate that the ASC can maintain a symmetric charge–discharge process with a slight discharge curve nonlinearity, which is attributed to the redox reaction (Fig. 7d). The energy and power densities are important parameters in ASC performance evaluation. A maximum energy density of 44.19 W h kg−1 is obtained at a power density of 397.75 W kg−1 (Fig. 7e); the former can be maintained at 30.03 W h kg−1 even at an extremely high power density of 8.315 kW kg−1, which is advantageous for many symmetrical supercapacitors, such as CuS-AC//AC (24.88 W h kg−1 at 800 W kg−1), [39] H–CuS//AC (15.97 W h kg−1 at 185.4 W kg−1), [40] CuS//AC-ASC (15.06 W h kg−1 at 392.9 W kg−1), [41] CuS/rGO//AC (21 W h kg−1 at 315 W kg−1), [42] Ti3C2/CuS//AC (15.4 W h kg−1 at 750.2 W kg−1), [43] CuS@CD-GOH//AC (28 W h kg−1 at 700 W kg−1), [44] B–CuS//AC (6.23 W h kg−1 at 1.75 kW kg−1), [45] and PIM/CuS@CNT//AC (4.54 W h kg−1 at 299 W kg−1) [35]. Fig. 7f presents the cycling stability of the CuS@CQDs-ICM//AC ASC obtained from the GCD measurements at a current density of 5 A g−1. The specific capacitance reaches 92.8% after 10,000 cycles. The system with the CuS@CQDs-SSM and CuS as the anode electrode and AC as the negative electrode is also evaluated. The CV and GCD curves of the CuS@CQDs-SSM//AC and CuS//AC ASC are displayed in Figs. S8 and S9. For demonstration, the CuS@CQDs-ICM//AC ASC is presented using different LED colors (Fig. S10). The results indicate that the CuS@CQDs-ICM//AC is an ideal material for supercapacitor electrodes.

In summary, a novel composite of CQD-doped CuS nanoflower is developed using SSM and ICM. The prepared composite material exhibits a nanoflower-like hollow structure with uniform size and no agglomeration. Given these unique structural features, the CuS@CQDs-ICM composite electrode produces a high specific capacitance of 920.5 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, which is greater than that of other previously reported metal S2−-based materials. The synergy between CuS and CQDs results in a long cycle life (i.e., capacitance retention of 92.8% after 10,000 cycles). Moreover, the high energy density of the CuS@CQDs-ICM composite (44.19 W h kg−1) is maintained up to 397.75 W kg−1. The developed material provides insights into the preparation of new and efficient electrode materials.

Yiling Quan: Visualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Guoxiang Wang: Conceptualization, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Funding acquisition, Validation.Lu Lu: Investigation.Zhixin Wang: Formal analysis. Hongfeng Xu: Software. Sa Liu: Data curation. Dazhi Wang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.21606033, 21376034 and 21506086) and Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (Grant number 2016J047)

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2020.136606.
